First Visualization And ggplot Basics

Goal

This notebook introduces the basic Day 1 visualization workflow.

We will build plots piece by piece:

  1. load data
  2. inspect the data
  3. start a plot object
  4. add one layer or change
  5. print the plot after each step
  6. interpret what the plot shows

This is the pattern we will use throughout the course.

The Grammar Of Graphics

A visualization combines:

  • data
  • aesthetic mappings
  • geometric objects
  • statistical transformations
  • scales
  • coordinates
  • facets
  • labels and themes

ggplot2 gives us a language for building those pieces in layers.

Load Data

We will use a cleaned public PRAMS dataset.

prams <- readr::read_csv("data/real/prams_2011_selected.csv")
Rows: 1222 Columns: 8
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): location_abbr, location, subgroup_cat, subgroup
dbl (4): depression_within_3_months_birth, anxiety_within_3_months_birth, bi...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
prams |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 1,222
Columns: 8
$ location_abbr                        <chr> "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR", "AR…
$ location                             <chr> "Arkansas", "Arkansas", "Arkansas…
$ subgroup_cat                         <chr> "Adequacy of Prenatal care", "Ade…
$ subgroup                             <chr> "ADEQUATE PNC", "INADEQUATE PNC",…
$ depression_within_3_months_birth     <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ anxiety_within_3_months_birth        <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth <dbl> 24.6, 12.6, 22.2, 36.0, 21.5, 24.…
$ alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth    <dbl> 53.5, 28.7, 40.9, 54.1, 45.8, 49.…

Each observation in this file is a pre-aggregated percentage for one location, subgroup category, and subgroup.

This is not person-level data.

Explore The Data Structure

Visualization starts before the first plot. First, use tables to understand what the plotted observations would represent.

How many records are in the file, how many locations are represented, and how many subgroup categories are included?

prams |>
  summarize(
    records = n(),
    locations = n_distinct(location),
    subgroup_categories = n_distinct(subgroup_cat)
  )
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  records locations subgroup_categories
    <int>     <int>               <int>
1    1222        26                  18

Which subgroup categories appear in the file?

subgroup_counts <- prams |>
  count(subgroup_cat, sort = TRUE)

subgroup_counts
# A tibble: 18 × 2
   subgroup_cat                                   n
   <chr>                                      <int>
 1 Maternal Age - 18 to 44 years in groupings   130
 2 Adequacy of Prenatal care                    104
 3 Income (years 2004 and beyond)               104
 4 Maternal Age (4 Levels)                      104
 5 Maternal Race/Ethnicity                      104
 6 Maternal Age (3 Levels)                       78
 7 Maternal Education                            78
 8 Birth Weight                                  52
 9 Marital Status                                52
10 Medicaid Recipient                            52
11 Mother Hispanic                               52
12 Number of Previous Live Births                52
13 On WIC during Pregnancy                       52
14 Pregnancy Intendedness                        52
15 Smoked 3 months before Pregnancy              52
16 Smoked last 3 months of Pregnancy             52
17 Maternal Age - 18 to 44 years only            26
18 None                                          26

How many estimates does each location contribute?

prams |>
  count(location, sort = TRUE) |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   location          n
   <chr>         <int>
 1 Arkansas         47
 2 Colorado         47
 3 Delaware         47
 4 Georgia          47
 5 Hawaii           47
 6 Maine            47
 7 Maryland         47
 8 Massachusetts    47
 9 Michigan         47
10 Minnesota        47

Each location appears many times because the file includes overall estimates and subgroup estimates. A plot of all records would mix those levels together.

The None subgroup category gives the overall estimate for each location. That is the right level for a first state/location comparison.

prams_overall <- prams |>
  filter(subgroup_cat == "None")

prams_overall |>
  select(
    location,
    location_abbr,
    binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth,
    alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth
  ) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  location      location_abbr binge_drinking_within_3_m…¹ alcohol_use_within_3…²
  <chr>         <chr>                               <dbl>                  <dbl>
1 Arkansas      AR                                   23.9                   49.6
2 Colorado      CO                                   26.7                   59.9
3 Delaware      DE                                   23.2                   54.9
4 Georgia       GA                                   12.6                   34.1
5 Hawaii        HI                                   24                     53.1
6 Massachusetts MA                                   24.5                   62.2
# ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth,
#   ²​alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth

For plots about overall location estimates, we will use prams_overall. For plots about the structure of the PRAMS file, we will use the full prams data.

This kind of data preparation is part of visualization. A plot can only answer the right question if the data are suited to the questions of interest. On Day 2 we will spend more time on the basic functions used to inspect, filter, summarize, and arrange data before plotting one variable at a time.

Plot 1: Distribution

Question: what is the distribution of reported binge drinking in the 3 months before birth?

For this first plot, use the overall location estimates. That way each plotted observation represents one state/location, not a subgroup slice.

prams_binge <- prams_overall |>
  filter(!is.na(binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth))

prams_binge |>
  select(location, location_abbr, binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  location      location_abbr binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth
  <chr>         <chr>                                        <dbl>
1 Arkansas      AR                                            23.9
2 Colorado      CO                                            26.7
3 Delaware      DE                                            23.2
4 Georgia       GA                                            12.6
5 Hawaii        HI                                            24  
6 Massachusetts MA                                            24.5

Start with data only.

p1 <- ggplot(data = prams_binge)

p1

Add an aesthetic mapping.

p2 <- ggplot(
  data = prams_binge,
  mapping = aes(x = binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)
)

p2

Add a geom.

p3 <- p2 +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)

p3

Add labels.

p4 <- p3 +
  labs(
    title = "Reported Binge Drinking In The 3 Months Before Birth",
    subtitle = "Overall PRAMS state/location estimates",
    x = "Percent reporting binge drinking",
    y = "Number of states/locations"
  )

p4

Add a theme.

p5 <- p4 +
  theme_minimal()

p5

Most overall state/location estimates are below 30 percent, but the distribution has a right tail with some higher estimates.

Plot 2: Compare Categories

Question: which states or locations have higher overall reported binge drinking estimates?

First, make a small table for the comparison.

prams_binge_ordered <- prams_binge |>
  arrange(desc(binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)) |>
  select(location, binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)

prams_binge_ordered |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   location     binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth
   <chr>                                       <dbl>
 1 Wisconsin                                    35.4
 2 Nebraska                                     33.4
 3 Minnesota                                    33.1
 4 Vermont                                      29.7
 5 Michigan                                     29  
 6 Wyoming                                      28.2
 7 Missouri                                     27.6
 8 Pennsylvania                                 27.5
 9 Maine                                        26.8
10 Colorado                                     26.7

Start with the ordered data and mappings.

prams_binge_ordered <- prams_binge_ordered |>
  mutate(location = reorder(location, binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth))

p1 <- ggplot(
  prams_binge_ordered,
  aes(x = location, y = binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)
)

p1

Use geom_col() because the bar height is already in the data.

p2 <- p1 +
  geom_col()

p2

Flip the coordinates so the location names are readable.

p3 <- p2 +
  coord_flip()

p3

Add labels and a theme.

p4 <- p3 +
  labs(
    title = "Reported Binge Drinking By Location",
    subtitle = "Overall PRAMS state/location estimates",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Percent reporting binge drinking"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4

The plot compares overall location estimates, not individual people. Ordering the locations makes the higher and lower estimates easier to see.

Plot 3: Association

Question: how are reported alcohol use and binge drinking related?

Use the same overall location estimates, then filter to places where both measures are observed.

prams_alcohol <- prams_overall |>
  filter(
    !is.na(alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth),
    !is.na(binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)
  )

prams_alcohol |>
  select(
    location,
    location_abbr,
    alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth,
    binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth
  ) |>
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  location      location_abbr alcohol_use_within_3_mont…¹ binge_drinking_withi…²
  <chr>         <chr>                               <dbl>                  <dbl>
1 Arkansas      AR                                   49.6                   23.9
2 Colorado      CO                                   59.9                   26.7
3 Delaware      DE                                   54.9                   23.2
4 Georgia       GA                                   34.1                   12.6
5 Hawaii        HI                                   53.1                   24  
6 Massachusetts MA                                   62.2                   24.5
# ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth,
#   ²​binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth

Start with data and mappings.

p1 <- ggplot(
  prams_alcohol,
  aes(
    x = alcohol_use_within_3_months_birth,
    y = binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth
  )
)

p1

Add points.

p2 <- p1 +
  geom_point()

p2

Add transparency so overlapping points are easier to see.

p3 <- p1 +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6)

p3

Add a trend line.

p4 <- p3 +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

p4
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Add labels and a theme.

p5 <- p4 +
  labs(
    title = "Alcohol Use And Binge Drinking In The 3 Months Before Birth",
    subtitle = "Each point is one overall PRAMS state/location estimate",
    x = "Percent reporting any alcohol use",
    y = "Percent reporting binge drinking"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p5
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

State/location estimates with higher reported alcohol use tend to have higher reported binge drinking. This is an association across pre-aggregated location estimates, not a person-level causal claim.

Create A Flag Before Plotting

Sometimes the variable we want to plot is not already in the data. Here, we can mark which locations are above the median binge-drinking estimate.

binge_median <- median(prams_binge$binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)

prams_binge_flagged <- prams_binge |>
  mutate(
    binge_group = if_else(
      binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth > binge_median,
      "Above median",
      "At or below median"
    ),
    location = reorder(location, binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth)
  )

prams_binge_flagged |>
  select(location, binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth, binge_group) |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 3
   location      binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth binge_group       
   <fct>                                        <dbl> <chr>             
 1 Arkansas                                      23.9 At or below median
 2 Colorado                                      26.7 Above median      
 3 Delaware                                      23.2 At or below median
 4 Georgia                                       12.6 At or below median
 5 Hawaii                                        24   Above median      
 6 Massachusetts                                 24.5 Above median      
 7 Maryland                                      22.1 At or below median
 8 Maine                                         26.8 Above median      
 9 Michigan                                      29   Above median      
10 Minnesota                                     33.1 Above median

Build a plot that uses the new flag for highlighting.

p1 <- ggplot(
  prams_binge_flagged,
  aes(
    x = location,
    y = binge_drinking_within_3_months_birth,
    fill = binge_group
  )
)

p1
p2 <- p1 +
  geom_col()

p2
p3 <- p2 +
  coord_flip()

p3
p4 <- p3 +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Above median" = "steelblue", "At or below median" = "gray75")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Reported Binge Drinking Above And Below The Median",
    subtitle = "Overall PRAMS state/location estimates",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Percent reporting binge drinking",
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4

The new variable does not change the estimates. It changes what the figure emphasizes by separating locations above the median from the rest.

Day 1 Pattern

The object-building pattern is:

p1 <- ggplot(data, aes(...))
p1

p2 <- p1 + geom_*()
p2

p3 <- p2 + labs(...)
p3

This makes it easier to see what each line of code contributes to the figure.

Tomorrow we will use this same pattern for counts, summaries, ordered categories, histograms, density plots, and unusual values.

Render Check

Render this notebook and confirm that each intermediate plot appears in the HTML output.
